speaker: interviewer
text: December 17, 1996. Interview with the survivor Maria Glickman nee Kuricki.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Ku-risky.'

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Ku-rit-ski.' The interviewer is Leslie Troper. We are in Springfield, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And the interview will be conducted in English. Today is December 17, 1996. This is Maria Glickman. I am Leslie Bennett-Troper, T-R-O-P-E-R, the interviewer. We are in Springfield, New Jersey, in the United States of America. And this interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Mrs. Glickman. Good morning to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria Glickman, G-L-I-C-K-M-A-N, M-E-R-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kuricki, Q-- K-U-R-I-C-K-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when and where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1924, in Vilna, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you begin by telling me about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember? The first that I remember is when my grandpa died. I was three years and eight months old. This was the first thing. And then I remember when my sister was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe those events.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1932. Her name was Asya Kuricki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about those incidents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing special, because we were too small to take us where. What I remember is my father got sick. He was 11 months in a hospital. He came out. And we lost everything in the bank. And he was a painter, artist painter. And you couldn't make a living. So he went on to work with the theater. He was an impresario. He took small children, bigger children, and he made them for dancers, singers. And he did go all of Poland. To every city that he came, he had a theater, and so he made a living. Not a bad one.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Szymon Kuricki. I have to spell it? No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me more about him.

speaker: interviewee
text: More about him. He was a person who liked to help people. He was-- in English, I don't know-- he was in the Jewish organizations, like [NON-ENGLISH], Beit Lehem, Hachnosas Orchim, Hachnosas Kallah. For the children who didn't have parents, he was one of the people who helped them out by getting money from the rich people for the poor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Those other groups that you mention, what kinds of groups were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're Jewish-- they were Jewish groups to-- Hachnosas Orchim, that means that somebody comes to the city, and he doesn't have where to sleep or what to eat, they put him in a place where he gets where to sleep and where to eat. [NON-ENGLISH] was a place, at Friday and the whole Saturday, the whole families will come, five, six, eight people. And they would get food Friday night, Saturday in the morning, Saturday in the middle of the day, and Saturday at night. And Hachnosas Kallah, this was for helping young people to get married, to get them work, to get them where to live. Then he was the vice president of a-- a hospital called-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not the Jewish-- I forgot the name.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city was the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vilna. We had two Jewish hospitals, the Jewish Hospital. And the other one, I don't remember the name. There were he was vice president. So he helped out for the poor people, through the Kehillah. Kehillah was the place where all the rich people sit together and think how to help the people who don't have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father belonged to them. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my mother helped out, too. When it was a concert, in the city, she would sit and sell the tickets. That's all. What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sonia Kuricki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: When she was younger, she was the director of all homes for the children who don't have parents, after the First War. And then she got married. And she didn't work anymore. She came from a very rich family. 99% of the family were doctors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your home growing up.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had actors, painters, writers, poets. I don't know. We had everybody. And everybody would come to see my father's pictures. Not what he did for people, just what he did for people to sell.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sold his paintings?

speaker: interviewee
text: He sold. But not too-- very much, because Vilna was a very poor city. We didn't have an industrial city. We had a couple of factories. That-- they had over 600,000 people, from them, 86,000 Jewish people. I know we had a couple of leather factories. We had boot factories. We had glass, too, glass factories. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the people had to live how they can. So everybody did what they could. It was a very hard life. I can't say that we had a hard life, but I can't say that we were millionaires. We weren't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother's father lost everything before he died. He had people working for him, 1,400 people. He had-- we call it [NON-ENGLISH]. That means old stuff is-- they bought-- they bought it, and then they sold it to factories, like in Bialystok, in Poland, in Lodz. In-- there were a couple of cities where they sold it. But he was old and sick, and he lost everything, just the house and that. That's all. And my father's father went terribly, in a terrible death, in 1918. Arath Kuricki Zalman got burned in his own house. My grandma did go to the market with the lady. And they came. No, my-- not my grandpapa, not-- not the house, nothing. When he-- he had burned alive when he was asleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: By whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By himself. He-- he smoked a pipe. Nobody touched him. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned a sister. Did you have any other siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a brother, Natan Kuricki, and a sister, Asya Kuricki. My brother got killed by the Germans. I don't know where. I don't know when. My sister and my mother runned, and they came to Siberia. They were there up to '45. I didn't know that they are alive up to '50-- '49. I haven't seen my mother, my sister, up to '58, 17 1/2 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we move to that part of the story, can you describe your childhood? Growing up with your brother and sister, what do you remember about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not nice, could be, to say. I was the love of my father and mother. Because when I was four and a half years old, my father took me to a ballet school. The boss of the ballet school was a Russian lady, around from Petersburg, Sabina Dol'skaia. And I was learning the ballet. I finished in '39, two and a half months before the war. I danced in the theater. I sang in the theater in Jewish. And they called me the wunderkind. And everybody did know me, because of my pictures. They were all over. I was very happy. But I didn't know-- this was June-- that in September 1, 19-- --20-- --39 will come out the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about going to school. What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved school. Not because I loved to learn. I'm telling. I loved the school because I was between children. I loved very much children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to public school or a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did go to Esther Rubinstein School. It was our minister, the Jewish minister, Rubinstein. That was in his wife's name, this school.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a wonderful school. We had to-- every morning, we had to sing the 'Hatikvah.' And that was just for girls not with boys together. And I loved it. What can I say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished school in '39. And the war break out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Russian came in. The first three days was terrible. They opened doors. They cut the pillows. They cut the blankets. They beat up people. They had the riot, the first three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then it got quiet. And for who it got good? It was for the communist people, who were communists by the Russian-- by the Polish people. So we lived a year and a half like this. And then the Russian came back. Describe the Jewish community before 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community? You see, we have five Jewish communities, one in the middle of the city and three-- four in all four sides of the-- of the city. And everybody spoke another Jewish language, had another intelligent. And it was-- we didn't know for sure how the people from the other side live. But what I know, through my father, is that most of the people were very poor but very intelligent. We are the people who gave out the first university, Jewish university in the world. We are the people who gave-- make from a jargon called, from a language that is taken from many languages. And we make one language, with grammar, to learn and to know what you learn about.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jewish. And this is the only university. We have it now in the 92nd Street and Fifth Avenue and Jewish. Everything was in Jewish. And then they were in Hebrew and Polish. We had 38 Jewish schools. This is the only city in the world. And because of this, we were called Jerusalem of Litha. And that's-- and we had wonderful doctors, engineers, professors. And they were wonderful, not just like doctors, like people. They always helped the poor. But because-- because there were many poor people. And I, myself, helped my girlfriend. [LAUGHS] She isn't here. I ought not talk about it. My mother teached me to always be good to people who don't have nothing. Can't remember everything, but I think this is the most that I did. When I-- when I got money for dancing, I didn't get the money. My father get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father would give me 10 zlotas and-- and 50 grozy. And I would take my poor girlfriend, and I would buy her food. And I bought her a coat once. [LAUGHS] I can't remember all of it anymore. What was the relationship between the Jews in your city and the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you. Me, I had friends not Jewish people. I loved the Polish people. And they loved me. And they helped me in wartime. If not them, I wouldn't be alive to go to the concentration camp. And in wartime, they helped the Jewish people more, because the Lithuanian people, when they came to Vilna-- they got it from Stalin-- they said, we are the bosses. And you will do what you want. So because of this, I think, the Polish people understood and helped the Jewish people. That's the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their attitude toward the Jews, in general, though, before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, we had the student-- we had one university in Vilna. And the students, they called them Endecja. They would stay by the Jewish stores and not allow not Jewish people to go in, in a store, and buy by Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a mandated law?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They did it by themselves. Nobody told them not to, because Pilsudski was dead. And his son-in-law was drunk, Marshal Rydz-Smigly. He was married to Pilsudski's daughter. And he didn't have time to look for his country. He had time to drink vodka. And this made very much trouble for the Polish people. This was the problem in Vilna. I can't say that somebody came and killed Jewish people or beat up Jewish people. There was one fall in '39. I did go from the movies with a friend of mine. I was 14 1/2 years old. And three students gave him a knife, here, in the right side.

speaker: interviewer
text: To you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my friend. They didn't touch me. I runned. And I runned to my father. And he came out with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think--

speaker: interviewee
text: He's alive. He is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that was an antisemitic action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course. They were the Endecja. The Endecja were anti-Semite. They were-- they worked for like with Hitler, not together but for Hitler.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language is Endecja?

speaker: interviewee
text: Endecja in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means fascist students.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other actions that you witnessed--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: --on the part of these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Never. Never.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your life begin to change?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the German came in. It was in '41, July 22. The war breaked out. And they came, 23, early in the morning, Monday.

speaker: interviewer
text: So three days, they came to look what we have. And they did go away. Then they came, and they took radios, like my father's pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had a-- a child in our house. And she was a child. She said, I'm not going to say that, in the attic, we have every radio and everything. So they went upstairs, and they brought down everything. And six weeks later, we were in the ghetto. Before the ghetto, I had to pay for electricity-- my father didn't go out-- me and my girlfriend, and they catched us. They didn't touch us. They just asked us to clean the offices, the places where they sleep. And they let us go, twice. That's all. And then they took us to the ghetto. Street by street, every street had police on horses, so nobody can run away. They took people to the ghetto and to the jail. My street and the second street of mine, did go to the ghetto, because it was straight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What street did you live on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pilsudskiego. This is the name of our general, who got the city back from-- from the Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the Germans came in, did you have any inclination-- did you know that there was anything-- did you know about the rise of Nazism outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father said that it will be war. And Hitler will kill every Jew that he can. But everybody would say, no, Kuricki, he's a special person, so he says so. But it came like he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had your father known about it? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the First War. And he was two years in a camp by the Germans. He spoke, perfectly, German. And he did know what they thinked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before, the First War, the German soldiers were the best friends of the Jewish people. You know why? Because they couldn't speak to nobody, just to the Jewish people, because the German language is very, very easily understood by the Jewish people and the opposite. So they spoke with the Jewish people. And they were friends. And they would help us. And not me, I wasn't alive. But they were good to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But after this, when the fang an, with the propaganda, that Hitler would come to, and he will be. He wasn't a German. He was an Austrian man. And he lived in Austria. But people think that he will give-- he said that there was 600,000 Jewish people in whole Germany. And he said that all of them are rich. And they have all the factories and all the money. And the German people believed him. So with this, he had to organize Gestapo, SD, Toten-battalion, and SS. And he organized them. And he came to Germany. And they make the Kristallnacht. They burned everything, the books, everything. And then they fang an to threw them to the concentration camps. Some run away. It was all kind of doing of his.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family know about these goings on in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- my-- you see, my mother-- we runned everybody to Russia. But the Russian didn't allow us to get through.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '41. So the-- my sister, with my mother-- she was a little one-- my mother hold her by the hand. And I don't know how they got through. They got through. And they catched them there. And they sent them straight to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: And me? We came back home. We had to sleep somewhere. We came back home. And six weeks later, they took us to the ghetto. And then we faengt an to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You started to describe the process by which they put the Jews into the ghetto. Could you continue that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Street by street-- one, two streets to the ghetto, two streets to the jail, Lukiszki, on a street [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we came in the ghetto 52,000 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't take nothing, because I was by my friend. And my father was alone. So what he did? He took two luggages, you know, with the girdle, and that's all. But something did get wrong, because one policeman couldn't stand that my father was, in 20 second, the prettiest man in Vilna.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave him with the-- the gun. No, not the gun, the flint. And my father was wearing pince-nez. You know what it means? No? Please tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pince-nez is not glasses. It's glasses with no frames and, here, a golden chain. But he had three of them. So two stayed there, and this was broken. So he gave him a cup of beaten up. And he walked in, in the ghetto. And I didn't know where he is. And he didn't know where I am. Then we find each other. And then we-- I had a girlfriend. She was living in the same place where the ghetto was. She is dead. She died in the-- Nobody killed her. She got tuberculosis, and she died. So we had a place in her apartment. This was two rooms and a kitchen. We were 38 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't. I did get to know them, when I was with them. But before, no. How could I? It's a big city and there are 86,000 people. You can't know everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who determined who was going to live in that apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody. We came. And we sit. And nobody said a word. Then, my girlfriend's sister was taken away and her mother-in-law. And then we were-- we were the bosses. And I stayed there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] up to '42, February 15 or 14. They catched me with five kilo potatoes. That means nine and a half pounds potatoes. And my girlfriend with five kilo of flowers. And they took me to Gestapo. And I was in Gestapo 11 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the potatoes?

speaker: interviewee
text: I bought them by the Polish people. I sell a dress of mine. And they gave me the potatoes. We didn't have money, not one penny. And then they send me, from the-- from the Gestapo, to the jail in Lukiszki. And there I was from the end of-- of February up to the beginning of May. I was between the 19 ladies, girls, and 19 men that officially were allowed, from the commandant of the SS, Weiss, to go out from the jail. The first one. All of the people who got took-- taken there, all of them got killed in Ponary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of the jail? [LAUGHS] Every-- first of all, the Germans. They said-- they looked like doctors. Everybody wearing a-- in a weiss-- a white shirt. And then it was the SS was around. [POINTING] And inside, food would give us the people who sit themselves, but they were not Jewish. And they helped us. They selled us bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the jail. In the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the jail was outside of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. We had a jail in ghetto, too, but this was so big. And this was a-- a-- a jail before the-- the war breaked out, I think 10, 15 years before the war. So they-- they selled us the bread. And they put it in the toilet where you put the papers. [POINTING] But we took that, because we were hungry. They gave us, twice a day, hot water, once a day, soup, , and 200 gram, big like this, bread. And the days were long. And we were hungry. And they allowed us to walk, every day, 20 minutes outside. Not outside the jail, outside the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop here and go onto the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
